scenarioName: Acute Inferior STEMI with Right-Sided Involvement

scenarioStory.history: 58-year-old male developed substernal chest pressure approximately 30 minutes ago while watching TV after dinner. Describes it as 'an elephant sitting on my chest,' radiating into the left jaw and down the left arm. Associated with diaphoresis, mild nausea, and a sense of impending doom — told his wife 'I feel like I'm gonna die.' No relief with position change or rest. Denies prior similar episodes. No recent illness, no trauma, no cocaine or stimulant use. Denies sildenafil, tadalafil, or any PDE5 inhibitor use within the last 48 hours.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: Crew arrives to find a 58-year-old male seated upright on the couch, pale and diaphoretic with a clenched fist held over his sternum. He speaks in full sentences but his voice is strained. Wife is at his side, visibly anxious. Patient is alert and oriented but uncomfortable, rating his pain 8/10. No respiratory distress at rest. Skin is cool and clammy. Radial pulse is strong and regular. → Right-sided leads acquired: Crew moves V4-V6 electrodes to the right precordium (V4R, V5R, V6R) and repeats the 12-lead. The repeat shows clear ST elevation in V4R — diagnostic of right ventricular involvement. Patient still uncomfortable, rating pain 8/10. BP unchanged. The crew now knows nitroglycerin is hemodynamically dangerous because RV stroke volume is preload-dependent. → BP slipping, careful bolus: Five minutes into transport, the monitor shows BP drifting downward — now 102/64 with HR creeping up to 92. Pt reports mild lightheadedness when the gurney was tilted for loading. Lungs remain clear (no pulmonary edema). This is RV failure declaring itself: low forward output from the stunned RV is starving the LV of preload. Crew must support preload with a careful NS bolus while continuing transport. → Stable arrival at PCI center: Patient delivered to the PCI-capable receiving facility with cath lab activated. BP recovered to 118/72 after 500 mL NS. HR 84, chest pain reduced to 3/10 after fentanyl, pt's mental status remains clear. Transferring care to the cath lab team — door-to-balloon clock already running thanks to pre-hospital STEMI alert.

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: Crew arrives to find a 58-year-old male seated upright on the couch, pale and diaphoretic with a clenched fist held over his sternum. He speaks in full sentences but his voice is strained. Wife is at his side, visibly anxious. Patient is alert and oriented but uncomfortable, rating his pain 8/10. No respiratory distress at rest. Skin is cool and clammy. Radial pulse is strong and regular.
relatedChecklist: Rapid primary survey (ABC); recognize suspected ACS presentation
Place on cardiac monitor, SpO2, full vitals
Acquire 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of contact
Obtain SAMPLE history; screen for PDE5 inhibitor use in last 48 hr
Aspirin 324 mg PO chewed (4 × 81 mg, non-enteric-coated preferred)
Establish IV access (18g antecubital preferred)

scenarioEvents.name: Right-sided leads acquired
scenarioEvents.description: Crew moves V4-V6 electrodes to the right precordium (V4R, V5R, V6R) and repeats the 12-lead. The repeat shows clear ST elevation in V4R — diagnostic of right ventricular involvement. Patient still uncomfortable, rating pain 8/10. BP unchanged. The crew now knows nitroglycerin is hemodynamically dangerous because RV stroke volume is preload-dependent.
relatedChecklist: Recognize ST elevation in V4R → RV infarct; mark NTG hemodynamically dangerous
Hold sublingual NTG given RV involvement and marginal preload reserve
Fentanyl 88 mcg IV slow push (88 kg × 1 mcg/kg; max 100 mcg)
Apply nasal capnography (early warning for opioid hypoventilation)
STEMI alert to receiving PCI center with pre-arrival ECG transmission
Initiate transport to PCI-capable center, position of comfort

scenarioEvents.name: BP slipping, careful bolus
scenarioEvents.description: Five minutes into transport, the monitor shows BP drifting downward — now 102/64 with HR creeping up to 92. Pt reports mild lightheadedness when the gurney was tilted for loading. Lungs remain clear (no pulmonary edema). This is RV failure declaring itself: low forward output from the stunned RV is starving the LV of preload. Crew must support preload with a careful NS bolus while continuing transport.
relatedChecklist: Recognize RV preload failure (drop in BP, clear lungs, no JVD rise)
NS 500 mL IV bolus, reassess; up to 30 mL/kg titrated to MAP 65
Reassess vitals q5 min; recheck lung sounds before each fluid bolus
Repeat 12-lead en route; watch for new AV block or rhythm change
Place defibrillator pads; configure VT/VF alert mode

scenarioEvents.name: Stable arrival at PCI center
scenarioEvents.description: Patient delivered to the PCI-capable receiving facility with cath lab activated. BP recovered to 118/72 after 500 mL NS. HR 84, chest pain reduced to 3/10 after fentanyl, pt's mental status remains clear. Transferring care to the cath lab team — door-to-balloon clock already running thanks to pre-hospital STEMI alert.
relatedChecklist: Structured hand-off: time of onset, ECG findings, meds, fluid given, pt response

scenarioEvents.name: NTG given, BP crashes
scenarioEvents.description: Crew administered NTG 0.4 mg SL without obtaining right-sided leads. Within 2-3 minutes BP drops precipitously from 148/86 to 76/44, HR climbs to 108 reflexively. Patient becomes obtunded, mumbling, slow to respond. Skin gray-pale and cold. Lungs remain CLEAR (no pulmonary edema) — the central distinguishing feature of RV-failure-induced hypotension, not LV failure. This is the consequence of treating an inferior STEMI without considering RV involvement.
relatedChecklist: Recognize NTG-induced preload collapse; obtain V4R now
Lay patient supine; passive leg raise to autotransfuse preload
NS 500 mL IV wide-open, reassess; repeat boluses up to 30 mL/kg
Continue boluses to MAP 65 — clear lungs after each bolus
If fluid-refractory hypotension persists: push-dose epinephrine 10 mcg IV q3-5 min
Expedite transport, advance STEMI notification with hypotension update
